content: Compound interest is a powerful force that can significantly grow your savings over time. It works by adding interest to your initial deposit, and then paying interest on that new, higher balance. This creates a snowball effect, where your savings grow faster and faster as time passes. For example, if you deposit $1,000 into a savings account earning 5% interest, you'll have $1,050 after one year. In the second year, you'll earn 5% interest on the new balance of $1,050, not just the original $1,000. This means you'll earn $52.50 in interest, bringing your total balance to $1,102.50. As the years go by, the difference can be staggering, with even modest savings growing into substantial sums. By starting to save early and being patient, you can harness the power of compound interest to achieve your long-term financial goals.
